source: Limitless (Bankless) — AI supercycle; Unity as world-model builder; chips/memory/power
summary: Orbital/space compute is an emerging frontier for AI infrastructure, with SpaceX as the critical enabler
summary_extended: Baker argues that orbital and space-based compute represents an emerging frontier for AI infrastructure, driven by terrestrial constraints on energy availability and land use that are increasingly limiting data center buildout. He contends that SpaceX's Starship rocket is the essential enabler for this vision, as only Starship's payload economics make it feasible to deliver server racks and energy infrastructure to orbit at scale — without it, the entire concept is non-viable. Baker frames this as an early-stage, speculative but strategically important thesis, representing a forward-looking shift in his thinking about where AI compute infrastructure ultimately goes. Because SpaceX is private and no pure-play public orbital compute company exists, Baker himself acknowledges limited actionability in public markets, which the hosts note is reflected in his actual 13F holdings. No specific numbers or timeframes were cited, but the implication is that this is a multi-year, long-duration structural theme to monitor rather than act on immediately.
detail: Baker has shifted focus toward orbital compute as a solution to terrestrial energy and land-use constraints on data centers. SpaceX (Starship) is the linchpin for delivering payload to orbit at scale. Without Starship, orbital data centers and energy-in-space are not viable. Baker sees this as early-stage but strategically important.
confidence_evidence: Described as a forward-looking focus shift; SpaceX is private; hosts note limited space-stock allocation in actual 13F suggesting Baker himself sees it as early
quote_fragment: orbital compute part, Starship required to get racks to orbit
